Course Parser

Paste a 15-week syllabus or existing course plan and Claude will propose a 7-module structure (6 modules × 2 weeks + 1 module × 3 weeks). Review and edit the proposal side-by-side, then download as a document to share with reviewers.

1. Source Content

▼

Pull content straight from the course's shared Google Drive folder, or import a single file / paste content directly below.

📁 Browse shared Google Drive folder
List the course's Drive folder and pick which files to pull into the source — supports Google Docs, DOCX, PDF, and TXT.
Files in folder
or import a single source
Drop a file here or click to browse
Supports .docx, .pdf, .txt — up to 10 MB
or paste directly

2. Review & Refine

Click any section to edit. Each module's row shows the original weeks it was built from, so you can review the mapping inline. Your edits save automatically.

Module Overview

Define topics and objectives for all modules. These will populate each module's detail page.

📐 Action Verbs & Cognitive Levels Expand for a guide to choosing the right verbs
▾

Use Bloom's taxonomy to pick the right verb for the level of thinking you're asking students to do.

1. Remember
define, list, recall, identify, name, recognize, state
2. Understand
classify, describe, explain, summarize, paraphrase, discuss
3. Apply
execute, implement, solve, use, demonstrate, compute, illustrate
4. Analyze
compare, contrast, differentiate, examine, organize, categorize
5. Evaluate
appraise, argue, assess, critique, defend, judge, justify, rank
6. Create
design, develop, formulate, construct, compose, generate, plan
🎯 Expected Learning Outcomes (ELOs) Reference for module alignment
▾

ELO Coverage Check

ELOs mapped to fewer than two MLOs may be under-developed. Map MLOs on this page (or in Course Mapping) to address them.

What happens next: Once you've defined topics and objectives here, we suggest you move onto the Assignments Overview in order to determine how your students will show what they've learned.

Course Mapping

ELO → MLO Alignment Matrix

Auto-populated from Module Overview tags

This matrix reflects the ELO tags you've assigned to each MLO on the Module Overview page. Edit tags there to update this matrix.

Alignment Audit

Run Audit generates a structural report — tracing ELOs through MLOs to activities and flagging gaps, orphans, or over-reliance. AI Double-Check reads the actual objective text and verifies that your ELO↔MLO tags make pedagogical sense.

Basic details about the course and instructor.

Course Description

Copy from the Course Catalog. This is the official catalog description.
Course Overview

Course Policies

These appear in the syllabus. Fill in what applies to your course.

Grading Policy
▾
The grade scale and rounding rule shown in the syllabus. Delete any rows your course doesn't use.
Late/Missing Work Policy
▾
Generative AI Usage Policy
▾
Pick a policy type to pre-fill standard language, then customize. Many colleges require the syllabus to state one of these.
Classroom Behavior Policy
▾
Expectations for classroom behavior, respectful communication, use of electronics, and recording devices.
Attendance Policy
▾
Include consequences, advance-notification expectations, and how students obtain missed information.

Course Objectives

Define what students will do and learn — start with Expected Learning Outcomes (ELOs), then derive Course Objectives (COs).

💡 Tips for writing objectives: Start each objective with one measurable action verb. Avoid vague verbs like "know," "understand," or "learn." A strong objective looks like: "Students will be able to [verb] [specific knowledge/skill] by [method or context]."
📐 Action Verbs & Cognitive Levels Expand for a guide to choosing the right verbs
▾

Learning objectives are organized into six levels of cognitive complexity — from recalling facts up to creating original work. Use this to pick the right verb for the level of thinking you're asking students to do. CLOs (Course Level Objectives) typically target lower-to-mid levels (understand, apply). ELOs (Expected Learning Outcomes) should use higher levels (apply, analyze, evaluate, create). MLOs (Module Learning Outcomes) can span any level depending on the module.

1. Remember — Recall facts & basic concepts
Verbs: define, list, memorize, recall, repeat, state, identify, name, recognize
2. Understand — Explain ideas or concepts
Verbs: classify, describe, discuss, explain, locate, recognize, report, select, translate, paraphrase, summarize
3. Apply — Use information in new situations
Verbs: execute, implement, solve, use, demonstrate, interpret, operate, illustrate, practice, compute
4. Analyze — Draw connections among ideas
Verbs: compare, contrast, differentiate, distinguish, categorize, deconstruct, examine, organize, relate, structure
5. Evaluate — Justify a stand or decision
Verbs: appraise, argue, assess, critique, defend, judge, justify, rank, recommend, support, validate
6. Create — Produce new or original work
Verbs: design, develop, formulate, construct, compose, generate, hypothesize, invent, plan, produce, synthesize
✨ Want personalized feedback? Open the AI Design Assistant to check your objectives against these cognitive levels, get verb suggestions, or understand how CLOs, ELOs, and MLOs relate.

Expected Learning Outcomes (ELOs)

Paste one objective per line (or a numbered/bulleted list — markers are stripped automatically). Each becomes its own ELO.

Course Objectives (COs)

Paste one objective per line (or a numbered/bulleted list — markers are stripped automatically). Each becomes its own CO.

Alignment Matrix

Check which ELOs support each CLO. An ELO can support multiple CLOs.

Textbooks & Required Materials

Course-wide required materials. Per-module resources go in each module's detail page.

💬 Leaving a comment? Highlight the relevant text in the document below, then click the Comment button that pops up. Your comments are saved and sent to the course designer.

Canvas Plan

Projected Canvas hierarchy · feeds CanvasCurate

Preview of how this course will land in Canvas once CourseCompose hands off to CanvasCurate. Each row is a module, page, activity, or material; the status badges show whether it's ready to build. Edits made elsewhere in the worksheet update this view automatically.

How this works ▾
  1. Compose — fill out the worksheet (Modules, Assignments, Materials, etc.). Each module gets a fixed page layout in Canvas: Overview → Learning Materials → Assignments.
  2. Watch the badges — every row here is a Canvas page or item Curate will build. Red flags block handoff; amber are worth checking but don't block. Click "Fix →" next to any reason to jump straight to the edit surface.
  3. Hand off — when blocking issues are clear, the 🚀 button lights up. It downloads a versioned bundle (coursecompose/v1.0) — spec + file manifest — that CanvasCurate ingests to build your Canvas modules and pages.
  4. Review the build — Curate returns a report (📥 Import build report). Each row picks up a status line: ✓ built · 🟠 needs manual touch · ⚠ errored. Open-in-Canvas links go straight to the live pages.
  5. Iterate — fix what Curate flagged, hand off again. History of the last 5 builds stays in the report card so you can see what changed.

✨ AI Design Assistant

Design Assistant
Hi! I can help you with course design. Here are some things I can do:

Bloom's Taxonomy: Check if your objectives use the right level of cognitive complexity.

COs → ELOs → MLOs: Help you understand the relationship between course-level and module-level outcomes.

Action Verbs: Suggest stronger, more measurable verbs for your objectives.

Alignment: Check that your activities actually assess what your objectives claim students will learn.

What would you like help with?
Check Bloom's level
Suggest ELOs
Check alignment
Review activities
